title: Miter Saw Cart
summary: A rolling plywood cart with fold-down leaves that support long boards level with the saw deck, plus lower shelves for storage.
description:
The single measurement that makes this cart actually useful is the cleat height: it has to sit exactly the distance down from the plywood's top edge that matches your own saw's deck height, so a board resting on the fold-out leaves sits flush with the saw table instead of tipping into it or sitting proud of it. Measure your specific saw before cutting anything — this number is not universal across brands or models.

The fold-down leaves are what let the cart double as a support table when the saw isn't in use, and also what let it collapse flat against a wall for storage. A second locking hinge support (beyond the plain shelf-hinge support) is worth adding if the leaves will also serve as an assembly table, since a single support isn't rated to hold a workpiece being pressed on or clamped.

materials.name: Plywood, 3/4", 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Ripped lengthwise into two 24" x 96" strips.

materials.name: Plate-mount caster wheels, with locking brakes
Shelf hinge supports, 12"
materials.note: A locking support is recommended if the leaves double as an assembly table.

cutList.note: Positioned to match your saw's deck height — measure before cutting.

steps.title: Measure your saw, then cut the parts
steps.body: Before any cut, measure the deck height of your own miter saw — the distance from the surface it sits on up to its table. That number is not on the cut list and is not universal across brands; every other measurement here follows from it. Then rip the 3/4" sheet lengthwise into two 24" x 96" strips and cross-cut two sides at 36", two shelves at 30" and two fold-down leaves at 29-3/4". Watch the yield: those six parts use 191-1/2" of the 192" the two strips give you, which leaves nothing for the saw kerf, so the six 24"-wide parts pack onto two 96" rips (one side + one shelf + one leaf per strip packs to exactly 96" with kerf) — one 4x8 sheet is enough if you lay them out that way. Cut four cleats at 24" from the 2x2 — four 24" cuts also consume a full 96" board with no kerf allowance — and four bottom trim supports at 31-1/2" from the 12 ft 1x3, which has plenty to spare.

steps.title: Mark and attach the cleats
steps.body: The goal is that your saw's table finishes exactly level with the top edge of the plywood sides, because that top edge is the line the fold-down leaves hinge from — get it right and a long board resting on the leaves sits flush with the saw table instead of tipping into it. Work the maths through, because it is easy to be 3/4" out: the saw sits on the shelf, and the shelf sits on the cleat, so the cleat's TOP face goes your saw's deck height PLUS the 3/4" shelf thickness down from the top edge of the side. Mark that distance on the inside face of both 36" sides, square a line right across, and screw a 24" 2x2 cleat along it running the full 24" front to back. Repeat lower down for the second shelf — there are four cleats, one per shelf per side — and mark both sides together so the shelves cannot come out sloped.

steps.title: Attach the shelves
steps.body: Stand the two sides parallel with their cleats facing in and set the 30" x 24" shelves onto them. The shelves run between the sides, so with 3/4" plywood at each end the cart finishes 31-1/2" wide overall — the same 31-1/2" as the bottom trim, which is the check that you have it the right way round. Screw down through each shelf into its cleats, then add 3/4" pocket holes with 1-1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue into the sides so each shelf is structurally fixed rather than just resting on a ledge. Check the box square across the diagonals before the glue sets: the leaves hinge off these sides, and a racked case gives you leaves that sit crooked no matter how the hinges are adjusted.

steps.title: Add the bottom trim
steps.body: Fit the 31-1/2" 1x3 bottom trim supports across the bottom of the cart, flush with the bottom edge — 31-1/2" is exactly the cart's outside width (30" between the sides plus 3/4" of side at each end), so they run the full front and back and lap onto the sides. Screw them into the sides and into the edge of the bottom shelf. Their real job is to triangulate the lower joint and give the caster plates something better than plywood edge grain to pull against. The lower shelf doubles as scrap wood storage, so keep the trim flush rather than proud if you want to slide offcuts in from the side.

steps.title: Mount the casters
steps.body: Turn the cart over and screw a plate-mount caster to each bottom corner, landing each plate on the 1x3 bottom trim and the plywood side rather than out on unsupported panel — set them in far enough that every screw in the plate is backed by wood. Set the cart down and check it doesn't rock before you trust it. Lock all four brakes before you ever run the saw: a miter saw's cut pushes the workpiece and the cart both, and a cart that rolls mid-cut is a spoiled cut at best.
materials: Plate-mount caster wheels, with locking brakes

steps.title: Attach the fold-down leaves
steps.body: Hang each 29-3/4" x 24" leaf on the outside of a side, hinged along the top edge of the plywood — the same edge the cleat height was set down from — so that a raised leaf lands level with your saw's table. Fit a 12" shelf hinge support under each leaf to carry it open and let it fold flat against the cart when it is not needed. Dry-fit one leaf and check it both folds flat without fouling the trim or casters and sits genuinely level when open, before you drill the second set of hinge screws. If the leaves will also serve as an assembly table, use a locking support: a plain shelf hinge is not rated for someone leaning on it or clamping a workpiece down.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand every surface and break the sharp edges, paying particular attention to the top edge of the sides and the outer edges of the leaves, since boards get dragged across both. Finish as you like — even a wiping oil or a single coat of poly on the shelves and leaves makes sawdust sweep off instead of clinging, and stops glue drips bonding permanently to the top.

images.alt: Rolling miter saw cart with fold-down support leaves
